item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this so much. This kind of stuff is profoundly fun to me. The dryness is exactly the tone needed for this kind of story.

+1 with enthusiasm

item.title: Re: A good idea, but there's far more potential here
item.content:encoded:
There is an SCP-000 page already (apart from the actual source of all anomalous behavior), which describes a being in an empty void that can create ripples of energy with voice.

SCP-000 is a tale, not an SCP article. There is no formal SCP-000 slot in the Series 1 listing.

What if the original SCP-000 merged with this idea?

The author of this article would likely need to confirm with the author of the SCP-000 tale first. Also, there's no reason to force the author of this page to incorporate material from the tale if the two pieces are unrelated and were never meant to be related.

item.title: A good idea, but there's far more potential here
item.content:encoded:
There is an SCP-000 page already (apart from the actual source of all anomalous behavior), which describes a being in an empty void that can create ripples of energy with voice.

What if the original SCP-000 merged with this idea? What if that being was the source? What if its ripples were the creation of anomalies? What if the abominations it sees are the people who understand it? What if the abomination that spoke "Foundation" to it is Dr. Cindi van Veenen?

There's potential for this to be a godlike SCP. I feel it's being left out.

Nevertheless, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had an idea like this but it literally hurt to process it. Considering what is natural and normal isn't normal and therefore is perceived as anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I try so hard not to do this, but I seriously couldn't make it all the way through this article. I've tried seven times, that I can remember. It's just wall after wall of dry, repetitive jargon. I never even made it to the hidden offset.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whenever I read about fantasy worlds, one question has always struck me. Humans are absolutely addicted to figuring out how things work, so where are all the people academically studying magic, trying to figure out how it works and where it's coming from? With the SCP-verse, this question has stuck with me even more, seeing as how the Foundation actually IS academically studying what is essentially magic. Where are the people trying to figure out what makes anomalies tick?

Well, I found them now.

Not only does this SCP answer that, it manages to also answer another pressing question at the same time - why do almost all GoIs seem to not know what morals are? Managing to answer two unrelated questions at once is incredibly impressive.

I love the scientific journal articles, I love the debating researchers, I love the lengths the Foundation is going to to hide this. The only part that could use some improvement is the ending - the list of anomalous objects is quite an anticlimax. However, that alone doesn't stop this from being one of my favorite SCPs.

Upvoted with passion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this idea, but holy shit, seeing all those drop downs scared me.

item.title: Re: Request to edit article slightly
item.content:encoded:
looks like you have to find the fragment pages.

Edit: After some profile lurking, this and this are the fragments.

item.title: Re: Request to edit article slightly
item.content:encoded:
Got it. Doing 1 and 4 rn.

Edit: OH GOD HOW DO YOU EDIT THIS?

item.title: Re: Request to edit article slightly
item.content:encoded: Not Staff, but the first and last edits are acceptable due to them being grammatical fixes. Edits 2 and 3 are stylistic changes (some people use he/she/other pronouns instead of 'it' and prefer not to use numbered lists) and would not be allowed.

item.title: Request to edit article slightly
item.content:encoded:
I'm only asking if I have permission to edit the article in some small, beneficial ways.

  1. Pluralize this word in the first addendum: "Or do you merely think that they are all results of our universe (or the whole of the multi-verse, for that matter) just being simply irrational?"
  2. Replace the current list in the first addendum with the Wikidot numbered list system (like this)
  3. Fix some small errors in the second addendum: "Did it create the universe, as Christians believes, or was the belief in him it (Remember: it's an object to the Foundation) that gave it life?"
  4. Add a missing word to this sentence in the seventh addendum: "As SCP-5128 is something that (apparently) cannot kill you if you do something stupid, the "privilege" of working on it, along with the importance it is taking thanks to Dr. van Veenen, is bound to create tensions among researchers, as there are some that do risk their lives everyday by researching and containing extremely deadly anomalies." (Speaking of which, isn't this already a problem with things like 999 and 682, for example?)

If not, then will someone who is allowed to edit please add these changes?
Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As many a wise author has said…

"If it doesn't add, it subtracts."

Another little thought: shouldn't this thing be Apollyon since it can make Apollyon anomalies? Danger is more important than usefulness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon'ed, _hard_. This explains my biggest gripe with the SCP setting as a whole: why the Foundation itself is the only organization that does not 'get' anomalies. And I feel like the pacing played out perfectly, too: when I was reading the first section, I thought it was all a bunch of philosophical bullshit that had no real bearing on reality, and how there was nothing rational about it. Except, of course, that was the POINT, and it set me up perfectly for the giant twist at the end. The real containment procedures are also one of the best approaches to misinformation I've ever seen on the site. The Foundation knew that nobody was going to want to leave this be, so they intentional approved it while also making it completely hopeless. It's devious, and I absolutely love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept behind this scip is good but it is filled with redundant jargon that ruins the mood to further read this entry.

I think it could be a great 001 proposal if done right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is a good article, uses a tone and language that would correspond to a scientific publication. I liked the manifesto, essays and publications presents, both scientific and philosophical, they feel like real papers, something that not many articles achieve.

At the same time, I enjoyed the debate generated among researchers. However, I may be missing something but I feel that it loses the element of mystery that was the basis of this contest, maybe this article worked even better as a proposal. At the same time the second part, which corresponds to the outcome, the explanation of the source of the anomalies does not convince me.

No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it. I just feel the ending is kind of dull and a bit underwhelming. I love the story you're telling, there's just no real impact for me unless I'm not missing some references from the A-01 to A-20 objects

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Take out the extremely lackluster containment and description off the first part and you'd have a good (if lengthy) tale about researchers debating theory.

That said, I agree with others who say it's long and dry, because it is. I agree the hidden offset didn't feel necessary. And I'm not a big fan of codifying anomalies to begin with, though I at least appreciate that the effect is mostly "person becomes obsessed with trying to find the origin of anomalies". Mostly, this is just too long for the payoff.

Also, it's very weird because there's an actual SCP-000 page already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much sums up my feelings about this. The whole "hidden offset" thing was entirely unnecessary and the piece would've been better had it not been present.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The science part was satisfyingly crunchy, but the tweest changed my original + to a - because it feels like the Foundation being dicks to its own researchers for the sake of it, given that the ability to manipulate the anomalous is, as the first part points out, not exactly rare or difficult to acquire for anyone who goes looking, and in addition to that, the Foundation having the ability to acquire an unlimited supply of Type Blue or Green individuals as necessary is something I'd expect the 05s' to collectively shit themselves with glee over, rather than gaslight and murder their staff because of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a hard time focusing throughout that first page. it was, tbh, kinda painful. but the second one is so darn good that it might really make up for it. and according to that second page, that was rather the point. the second page was riveting. still, ouch. based on that im keeping my vote neutral for now. there's a solid chance upvote before the contest ends.

here's a solid chance upvote before the contest ends. have GOT to hit the sack but there was some stilted phrasing and such that I noticed and wrote down in a draft, I can share them tomorrow unless the author doesn't want me to. maybe slightly nitpicky but I'm truly just trying to be helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great. I love the implication that obsessing over where anomalies come from creates anomalies. I love the researcher dialog. I love how the length and obfuscation of the first part page iteration is explained with the second page iteration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the back-and-forth between the researchers was interesting and compelling. I did not think the same of the wall of scientific research text or the hidden true SCP file.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The most real-looking document in the site (at least as far as I'm aware of). Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip is pretty fascinating! I like how it feels like an actual scientific report. Big heads up though, it shouldn't be "Thaumiel", as that Class is used only for objects that aid the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I heard you guys like science.

So how about we try to science our way into the Foundation's greatest mystery?

Preferred alternative slots:
5128
5364
5587
5847
5934
